title: The 'when' Expression
description: Kotlin's supercharged version of the switch statement.

slides.title: Too Many Else Ifs 😖
slides.content:
## A Better Way

Remember our `if / else if` chain from an earlier lesson? If you have 10 possible conditions, typing `else if` 9 times gets really ugly and hard to read.

Kotlin gives us a beautiful alternative: the **`when` expression**.

slides.title: Basic When 🚦
slides.content:
You pass a variable into `when()`, and it checks the value against a list of possibilities using the `->` (arrow) symbol.

The `else` acts as the safety net if none of the specific values matched.

quizData.question: What does a comma (,) do inside a `when` branch, like `2, 3 ->` ?

options.text: It means the branch triggers if the variable is equal to 2 OR 3.
It means the variable must be between 2 and 3.

quizData.explanation: A comma allows multiple cases to trigger the exact same block of code.

slides.title: Why 'when' instead of 'switch'?
slides.content:
## Supercharged 🔋

If you know Java or C++, `when` is Kotlin's version of `switch`. But it's far more powerful.

You don't need to manually type `break` statements. Once a matching branch is found and executed, the `when` block cleanly exits on its own!

quizData.question: Do you need to write a `break` command inside a `when` branch to stop it from running the options below it?

options.text: No, Kotlin's `when` automatically stops after the first successful match.
No, it just continuously runs all of them.

quizData.explanation: Unlike Java's `switch`, Kotlin's `when` does not have 'fallthrough'. It safely exits once a match executes.

slides.title: When as Expressions ✨
slides.content:
Just like `if`, `when` can also be used as an expression, assigning its result directly into a variable!

Note: If you use it this way, you **MUST** provide an `else` branch.
slides.codeSnippet:
val day = 5

val greeting = when (day) {
  1 -> "Ugh, Monday."
  5 -> "TGIF!"
  6, 7 -> "Weekend vibes!"
  else -> "Just another day."
}

println(greeting)
